title: Interstate 490 (New York)

* [I-90 Toll](/wiki/Interstate_90_(New_York) "Interstate 90 (New York)") / [New York Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") in [Victor](/wiki/Victor_(town),_New_York "Victor (town), New York")

[Genesee](/wiki/Genesee_County,_New_York "Genesee County, New York"), [Monroe](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York")

(**I-490**) is an [auxiliary Interstate Highway](/wiki/Auxiliary_Interstate_Highway "Auxiliary Interstate Highway") that serves the city of [Rochester, New York](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"), in the United States. It acts as a northerly alternate route to the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") ([I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_New_York "Interstate 90 in New York")), leaving it at exit 47 in the town of [Le Roy](/wiki/Le_Roy,_New_York "Le Roy, New York") and rejoining the highway at exit 45 in the town of [Victor](/wiki/Victor,_New_York "Victor, New York") 37.4 miles (60.2 km) to the east. I-490 connects with [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") and [New York State Route 390](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_390 "New York State Route 390") (NY 390) on the western side of Rochester and [I-590](/wiki/Interstate_590 "Interstate 590") and [NY 590](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_590 "New York State Route 590") on the east side of the city at an interchange known as the [Can of Worms](/wiki/Can_of_Worms_(interchange) "Can of Worms (interchange)"). The highway comprises the southernmost portion of the [Inner Loop](/wiki/Inner_Loop_(Rochester) "Inner Loop (Rochester)"), a beltway around the interior of Rochester. Outside the city, I-490 serves several suburban villages, such as [Churchville](/wiki/Churchville,_New_York "Churchville, New York") and [Pittsford](/wiki/Pittsford_(village),_New_York "Pittsford (village), New York").

**Interstate 490** (**I-490**) is an [auxiliary Interstate Highway](/wiki/Auxiliary_Interstate_Highway "Auxiliary Interstate Highway") that serves the city of [Rochester, New York](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"), in the United States. It acts as a northerly alternate route to the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") ([I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_New_York "Interstate 90 in New York")), leaving it at exit 47 in the town of [Le Roy](/wiki/Le_Roy,_New_York "Le Roy, New York") and rejoining the highway at exit 45 in the town of [Victor](/wiki/Victor,_New_York "Victor, New York") 37.4 miles (60.2 km) to the east. I-490 connects with [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") and [New York State Route 390](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_390 "New York State Route 390") (NY 390) on the western side of Rochester and [I-590](/wiki/Interstate_590 "Interstate 590") and [NY 590](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_590 "New York State Route 590") on the east side of the city at an interchange known as the [Can of Worms](/wiki/Can_of_Worms_(interchange) "Can of Worms (interchange)"). The highway comprises the southernmost portion of the [Inner Loop](/wiki/Inner_Loop_(Rochester) "Inner Loop (Rochester)"), a beltway around the interior of Rochester. Outside the city, I-490 serves several suburban villages, such as [Churchville](/wiki/Churchville,_New_York "Churchville, New York") and [Pittsford](/wiki/Pittsford_(village),_New_York "Pittsford (village), New York").

The eastern half of the freeway, named the **Eastern Expressway**, was built in stages from the 1950s to the 1970s as a connector between the Inner Loop and the thruway, and the section west of the Inner Loop and [Downtown Rochester](/wiki/Downtown_Rochester "Downtown Rochester") to I-90 in Le Roy is known as the **Western Expressway**. From Downtown Rochester to the Can of Worms, it follows the former [right-of-way](/wiki/Right-of-way_(transportation) "Right-of-way (transportation)") of the [Rochester subway](/wiki/Rochester_subway "Rochester subway") and, before it, the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"). The section west of the Inner Loop was mostly built during the 1960s and completed in the early 1970s. During the 1950s and early 1960s, the portion of the Eastern Expressway from what is now the Can of Worms east to [Bushnell's Basin](/wiki/Bushnell%27s_Basin,_New_York "Bushnell's Basin, New York") was originally designated as part of [NY 96](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_96 "New York State Route 96"). That route was moved back onto its parallel surface routing c. 1961 when I-490 was assigned to the entirety of the then-proposed Le Roy–Victor freeway.

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_490_(New_York)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/I490West.jpg/250px-I490West.jpg)](/wiki/File:I490West.jpg)

I-490 eastbound west of Downtown Rochester in the final stages of the Western Gateway project

Heading northeast from exit 47 of the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") ([I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_New_York "Interstate 90 in New York")), I-490 passes through rural portions of eastern [Genesee County](/wiki/Genesee_County,_New_York "Genesee County, New York") and western [Monroe County](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York"), skirting the villages of [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen_(village),_New_York "Bergen (village), New York") and [Churchville](/wiki/Churchville,_New_York "Churchville, New York"). Gradually, the expressway takes a more easterly alignment near exit 3 before returning to the northeast at exit 4. At exit 6, I-490 intersects the Airport Expressway ([NY 204](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_204 "New York State Route 204")). Past this interchange, I-490 heads due north, connecting to [NY 33](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_33 "New York State Route 33") and [NY 531](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_531 "New York State Route 531") before returning east. Prior to crossing the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"), I-490 meets [NY 390](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_390 "New York State Route 390") and [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390"). Beyond the junction lies the canal and the city of [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Between the Mount Read Boulevard interchange at exit 10 and the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River"), I-490 is referred to as the "Western Gateway". This section, which saw major decorative and structural improvements in the late 2000s,[[7]](#cite_note-7) travels due east through heavily residential neighborhoods before turning to the southeast near [ESL Ballpark](/wiki/ESL_Ballpark "ESL Ballpark") and a junction with the [Inner Loop](/wiki/Inner_Loop_(Rochester) "Inner Loop (Rochester)") at exit 13. Just west of this point, I-490 passes over West Broad Street ([NY 31](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31 "New York State Route 31")) and close to the former Rochester terminal of the [Buffalo, Rochester and Pittsburgh Railway](/wiki/Buffalo,_Rochester_and_Pittsburgh_Railway "Buffalo, Rochester and Pittsburgh Railway"), which now houses [Nick Tahou Hots](/wiki/Nick_Tahou_Hots "Nick Tahou Hots").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

I-490 now becomes part of the Inner Loop as it passes just south of the city center and heads toward the Genesee River. I-490 crosses both the river and [NY 383](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_383 "New York State Route 383") by way of the [Frederick Douglass–Susan B. Anthony Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Frederick_Douglass%E2%80%93Susan_B._Anthony_Memorial_Bridge "Frederick Douglass–Susan B. Anthony Memorial Bridge") and connects to [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15") before leaving the Inner Loop[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and turning south and east to follow the former pathway of the Erie Canal[[8]](#cite_note-1920topo-8) and the [Rochester subway](/wiki/Rochester_subway "Rochester subway") through the east side of the city.[[9]](#cite_note-subway-9) Along this stretch, I-490 connects to NY 31 (now part of Monroe Avenue) and passes north of [Cobbs Hill Reservoir](/wiki/Cobbs_Hill_Reservoir "Cobbs Hill Reservoir") and the surrounding [Cobbs Hill Park](/wiki/Cobbs_Hill_Park "Cobbs Hill Park").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/ROC_CanofwormsEast.jpg/250px-ROC_CanofwormsEast.jpg)](/wiki/File:ROC_CanofwormsEast.jpg)

I-490 westbound at the Can of Worms

I-490 continues to run in the former bed until exit 21,[[8]](#cite_note-1920topo-8) where I-490 connects to [NY 590](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_590 "New York State Route 590") and [I-590](/wiki/Interstate_590 "Interstate 590") at an interchange known locally as the [Can of Worms](/wiki/Can_of_Worms_(interchange) "Can of Worms (interchange)").[[10]](#cite_note-10) At this point, the former Erie Canal route (now part of I-590) curves southward[[8]](#cite_note-1920topo-8) while I-490 continues eastward into the eastern suburbs of Rochester. Between exits 21 and 24, I-490 parallels the [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation")-owned [Rochester Subdivision](/wiki/Rochester_Subdivision "Rochester Subdivision") rail line, intersecting [NY 441](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_441 "New York State Route 441"), a four-lane [divided highway](/wiki/Divided_highway "Divided highway") in the process. South of exit 25, I-490 traverses the southeastern suburbs of Rochester, passing close to [East Rochester](/wiki/East_Rochester,_New_York "East Rochester, New York"), [Pittsford](/wiki/Pittsford_(village),_New_York "Pittsford (village), New York"), and [Bushnell's Basin](/wiki/Bushnell%27s_Basin,_New_York "Bushnell's Basin, New York") and closely paralleling [NY 96](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_96 "New York State Route 96") on its way toward the [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York") line. The freeway meets NY 96 twice in [Perinton](/wiki/Perinton,_New_York "Perinton, New York") and a third time in the Ontario County town of [Victor](/wiki/Victor,_New_York "Victor, New York"), where I-490 passes along the western fringe of [Eastview Mall](/wiki/Eastview_Mall "Eastview Mall"). I-490 ends a short distance southeast of the third NY 96 interchange at Thruway exit 45.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_490_(New_York)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The portion of I-490 from exit 15 southeast to the [Can of Worms](/wiki/Can_of_Worms_(interchange) "Can of Worms (interchange)") follows the original path of the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") through the city of [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York").[[8]](#cite_note-1920topo-8) After the canal was rerouted to bypass Rochester in 1920, the former canal bed was purchased by the city for roughly $1.5 million (equivalent to $17.7 million in 2024[[11]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-11)).[[9]](#cite_note-subway-9) Plans drawn up by the city in the early 1910s called for a highway to be built in the old canal bed; however, subsequent proposals leaned toward repurposing the bed as a rapid transit system instead. The [Rochester subway](/wiki/Rochester_subway "Rochester subway"), as it became known, began operation in 1927. As ridership on the line declined in the 1940s and early 1950s, the city elected to shut the subway down in 1956 and use the [right-of-way](/wiki/Right-of-way_(transportation) "Right-of-way (transportation)") for a new highway connecting the [Inner Loop](/wiki/Inner_Loop_(Rochester) "Inner Loop (Rochester)") to the recently completed [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") south of Rochester.[[9]](#cite_note-subway-9)

Construction of the Eastern Expressway, a [limited-access highway](/wiki/Limited-access_highway "Limited-access highway") connecting the Inner Loop to the thruway in [Victor](/wiki/Victor,_New_York "Victor, New York"), began in the early 1950s with the first section extending from [NY 96](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_96 "New York State Route 96") in [Bushnell's Basin](/wiki/Bushnell%27s_Basin,_New_York "Bushnell's Basin, New York") to [NY 31F](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31F "New York State Route 31F") near [East Rochester](/wiki/East_Rochester,_New_York "East Rochester, New York").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) It was completed by 1956 and originally designated as part of NY 96.[[14]](#cite_note-14) An extension northwest to the present site of the Can of Worms was opened to traffic by the following year.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The expressway remained part of NY 96 until c. 1961 when it was designated as part of I-490, a proposed route extending westward through Downtown Rochester and southwestward through the western suburbs to Thruway exit 47 in [Le Roy](/wiki/Le_Roy,_New_York "Le Roy, New York"). The portion of the highway between the [Inner Loop](/wiki/Inner_Loop_(Rochester) "Inner Loop (Rochester)") and Winton Road was completed in the old subway cut by this time,[[3]](#cite_note-1960map-3)[[4]](#cite_note-1961map-4) while the segment between Winton Road and the Can of Worms was opened c. 1963.[[16]](#cite_note-1962map-16)[[17]](#cite_note-1963map-17)

Work on the portion of I-490 west of the Inner Loop began c. 1962 and initially extended from [NY 259](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_259 "New York State Route 259") in [Chili](/wiki/Chili,_New_York "Chili, New York") to Mount Read Boulevard two miles (3.2 km) west of downtown.[[4]](#cite_note-1961map-4)[[16]](#cite_note-1962map-16) This section was completed by the following year.[[17]](#cite_note-1963map-17) The remainder of the freeway west of Rochester was opened to traffic as far west as [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") near [Churchville](/wiki/Churchville,_New_York "Churchville, New York") c. 1965[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) and finished by 1968.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The last two gaps in the freeway—from Mount Read Boulevard east to the Inner Loop in Rochester and from Bushnell's Basin southeast to the thruway in [Pittsford](/wiki/Pittsford,_New_York "Pittsford, New York") and Victor—were filled in the early 1970s.[[5]](#cite_note-1969map-5)[[6]](#cite_note-1971map-6)

I-490 formerly had two sets of [rest areas](/wiki/Rest_area "Rest area"), one in [Victor](/wiki/Victor,_New_York "Victor, New York") and one in Churchville. The Churchville pair, located east of exit 3, were closed on July 18, 1990, after numerous complaints about sexual activity in the area.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The ones in Victor, west of the thruway interchange, were closed on November 1, 1990, for the winter as a temporary cost-saving measure.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") decided in June 1991 to make these closures permanent to save on costs.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The planned construction of the Inner Loop (now part of I-490) through the Corn Hill district of Downtown Rochester just west of the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") was the driving factor that led the [Rochester Institute of Technology](/wiki/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology "Rochester Institute of Technology") (RIT) to relocate to its present location in [Henrietta](/wiki/Henrietta,_New_York "Henrietta, New York") in 1968. The plan called for the demolition of a number of RIT buildings and would have resulted in splitting the campus into two halves separated by the new freeway.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) The portion of I-490 from exit 9 ([I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") and [NY 390](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_390 "New York State Route 390")) in [Gates](/wiki/Gates,_New_York "Gates, New York") to exit 27 (NY 96) in [Perinton](/wiki/Perinton,_New_York "Perinton, New York") was ceremoniously designated as the "Erie Canal Expressway" by the [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature") on August 16, 2005.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
